coredata.dc:title: pH-induced aggregation of hydrophilic carbon dots for fluorescence detection of acidic amino acid and intracellular pH imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yu Zhu
Xiao, Na
Liu, Shi Gang
Han, Lei
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: Intracellular pH level plays an important role in physiological and pathological processes. The development of nanoprobes for detecting in vivo pH levels is especially important for early diagnosis of disease. Therefore, we develop a hydrophilic carbon points (CDs) using quercetin and ethylenediamine as precursors to monitor intracellular pH. Under optimized conditions, the prepared CDs not only have uniform particle size and morphology, but also possess strong green fluorescence, photostability, and photoreversibility in water medium. The CDs exhibit pH-sensitive fluorescence effect under acidic and alkaline conditions, which is used to achieve “off-on-off” detection pH (from 3.5 to 13.5). Meanwhile, the pH-dependent mechanism is further investigated and explained, which is the fluorescence quenching caused by the pH-induced aggregation. Based on the pH-sensitive characteristics of CDs, it has been applied to the detection of aspartic acid and glutamic acid. More importantly, when applied to live cells, the pH-probe exhibits low cytotoxicity and high sensitivity, and is successfully used in intracellular pH fluorescence imaging. Consequently, this nanoprobe is expected to be used for real-time monitoring of intracellular pH level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
pH-induced aggregation
Acidic amino acid
Fluorescence nanoprobe
Intracellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of pH and acidic amino acids
Optical properties and characterization of CDs
Mechanism of fluorescence “off-on-off” method for determination of pH
Selectivity of CDs fluorescent pH nanoprobe
Methylthiazol tetrazolium assay testing and intracellular pH imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Excitation (ex), emission (em), and UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs in PBS buffer (pH 7.4); and corresponding photographs of solution: (a) under visible light and (b) UV light at 365 nm. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at excitation wavelengths from 340 to 430 nm. (C) TEM image of CDs and HRTEM image of CDs with lattice (inset). (D) TEM image of CDs and size distribution in pH 7.4 PBS buffer (inset).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the pH range of 3.5–7.4. Inset: corresponding photographs of solutions under UV light (365 nm). (B) The fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F7.4) of CDs as a function of pH in the range of 3.5–7.4. (C) Fluorescence spectra of CDs within the pH range of 7.4–12.5, inset: corresponding photographs of solutions under UV light (365 nm). (D) The fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F7.4) of CDs as a function of pH in the range of 7.4–13.5.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs solution, curves 0, 1, and 2 represent original CDs solution (pH 7.4), pH of the solution is adjusted from 7.4 to pH 4.5 and then adjusted to pH 7.4. Inset: corresponding photographs of solutions under UV lamp. (B) Fluorescence reversibility against pH change between 4.5 and 7.4, repeatedly. (C) Fluorescence spectra of CDs solutions. Curves 0, 1, 2 represent the original CDs solution (pH 7.4), pH 12.5 solution adjusted from pH 7.4, and pH 7.4 solution adjusted from pH 12.5. Inset: corresponding photographs of solutions under UV lamp. (D) Fluorescence reversibility against pH change between 12.5 and 7.4, repeatedly.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs in aqueous solutions at pH 7.4, 4.5, and 12.5. (B) TEM image of CDs, inset: size distribution in PBS (pH 4.5). (C) TEM image of CDs, inset: size distribution in PBS (pH 12.5). (D) The mechanism diagram of pH-induced aggregations of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of exogenous cations and anions (50 μM) at PBS pH 7.4. (B) Fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of exogenous amino acids (50 μM) at PBS pH 7.4. (C) Fluorescence intensity of CDs at different concentrations of Asp in water (0.01–0.5 mM). (D) Stern–Volmer plot of CDs at different concentrations of Asp, inset: the corresponding linear relationship at low concentrations of Asp (0.01–0.1 mM). (E) Fluorescence intensity of CDs at different amounts of Glu in water (0.01–0.5 mM). (F) Stern–Volmer plot of CDs at different concentrations of Glu, inset: the corresponding linear relationship at low amounts of Glu (0.01–0.1 mM).

(A) CLSM images of HepG2 cells treated with the CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) at different pH values. (B) The fluorescence intensity obtained by scanning individual cells at different pH values (n = 3). Scale bar: 20 μm.
Principle of the reaction between quercetin and ethylenediamine, and schematic of synthesis and purification of CDs.

head.ce:title: pH-induced aggregation of hydrophilic carbon dots for fluorescence detection of acidic amino acid and intracellular pH imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Teaching, Zunyi Medical and Pharmaceutical College, Zunyi 563006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Basic Teaching, Zunyi Medical and Pharmaceutical College, Zunyi, 563006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intracellular pH level plays an important role in physiological and pathological processes. The development of nanoprobes for detecting in vivo pH levels is especially important for early diagnosis of disease. Therefore, we develop a hydrophilic carbon points (CDs) using quercetin and ethylenediamine as precursors to monitor intracellular pH. Under optimized conditions, the prepared CDs not only have uniform particle size and morphology, but also possess strong green fluorescence, photostability, and photoreversibility in water medium. The CDs exhibit pH-sensitive fluorescence effect under acidic and alkaline conditions, which is used to achieve “off-on-off” detection pH (from 3.5 to 13.5). Meanwhile, the pH-dependent mechanism is further investigated and explained, which is the fluorescence quenching caused by the pH-induced aggregation. Based on the pH-sensitive characteristics of CDs, it has been applied to the detection of aspartic acid and glutamic acid. More importantly, when applied to live cells, the pH-probe exhibits low cytotoxicity and high sensitivity, and is successfully used in intracellular pH fluorescence imaging. Consequently, this nanoprobe is expected to be used for real-time monitoring of intracellular pH level.

ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular microenvironment pH plays a key role in some pathological and physiological processes [1–3]. In normal cells, intracellular pH is adjusted by the cation-anion transporters on the cell membrane to make it nearly neutral [4,5]. Specifically, intracellular pH microfluctuation can lead to cell proliferation, cell migration and metabolism [6]. For example, many scientific studies have revealed that abnormal pH values can be used as markers for cancer, and monitoring intracellular pH may provide a basis for early diagnosis of cancer [7,8]. As another example, changes of mitochondrial pH are closely related to signs of mitochondrial apoptosis [9]. It can be seen that small changes in intracellular pH may cause complex cellular microenvironment changes. In other words, the accurate detection of intracellular pH level is important for the early diagnosis and physiological diseases, and is also confronted with huge challenge. Thus, it is urgent and important to develop a practical and accurate fluorescent pH-sensitive probe for pH monitoring in vivo.

Amino acids are the basic units of the constituent protein, the material basis of promoting growth, normal metabolism and maintaining life. Aspartic acid (Asp) and glutamic acid (Glu) are two acidic amino acids. They play a significant role in the formation and growth of organisms [10]. Recent studies have shown that abnormal Asp levels may be used as a basis for early diagnosis of tumors [10]. Up to now, there have been many reports on amino acid-based detection [11,12]. However, these materials have the disadvantage of poor selectivity, mainly because amino acids have the same functional groups of amino and carboxyl. Therefore, the development of a sensitive and highly selective amino acid probe is still a challenge and an urgent problem to be solved.

Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent nanomaterials, which are a zero-dimensional semiconductor nanocrystal and possess approximately spherical structure with a diameter of less than 10 nm. CDs have widely attracted researchers due to their advantages such as suitable size, low cost, and easy preparation. The main preparation methods of CDs include electrochemical synthesis [13,14] microwave-assisted synthesis [15,16] and hydrothermal methods [17,18]. Compared with metal quantum dots, the CDs have the advantages of low toxicity, low environmental hazard, photostability, good water solubility and biocompatibility [19–21] It has been applied widely to many fields including bioimaging [22–24], photocatalysis [25,26], biological analysis [27–30], drug carrier [31,32], and environmental monitoring [33–35]. At present, the reported CDs as a pH fluorescence nanoprobe have been used for intracellular pH regulation [36], pH-triggered administration [37,38], pH biosensing [39–43] and so on. Among these works, the pH sensitivity of CDs is based on protonation and deprotonation [44,45] and pH-dependence of surface defects [46]. Most of the pH-dependent mechanisms are explained by the fact that carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups contained on the surface of CDs are prone to protonation and deprotonation with solution or pH-induced surface defect effects of CDs, causing pH-sensitive fluorescent properties [16,47]. However, there are rare reports on pH nanoprobes on the basis of the pH-induced aggregation quenching.

In this work, we developed CDs to achieve pH fluorescence “off-on-off” detection by using pH-induced aggregation effect. Quercetin and ethylenediamine were chosen as the precursors and one-step hydrothermal method was used to successfully prepare the CDs. The prepared crude product was purified by column chromatography to obtain green fluorescent CDs. The spectral properties and structural characterization of CDs were studied. The CDs displayed photoreversibility, pH-sensitive property and excellent photostability. When the pH is raised to 7.4 from 3.5, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually increases, whereas it gradually decreases in the range of 7.4 to 13.5, and shows a good linear relationship, respectively. Using the unique pH-sensitive property, the CDs achieved fluorescence “off-on-off” detection of pH. Moreover, based on the pH sensitivity of CDs, and Asp/Glu isoelectric point properties, the pH-nanoprobe was used to detect acidic amino acids, and the desired results were obtained. In addition, the CDs had good biocompatibility via toxicity analysis. More importantly, the pH-sensitive nanoprobe was exploited to monitor the cellular pH level and intracellular imaging.

Quercetin, ethylenediamine, KH2PO4, Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaCl, and other salts were obtained from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutamic acid (Glu), aspartic acid (Asp), and other amino acids were obtained from Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used as the solvent. Column chromatography silica gel (200–300 mesh) was purchased from Qingdao Marine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shan Dong, China).

The pH values of solutions were tested using a PHS-3C pH meter (Leici, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was implemented on an ESCALab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured with a UV–Vis 2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F-2700 spectrofluorophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Bruker IFS 113 v spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were carried out on a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscopy (FEI, USA). The Zeta-potential measurements were carried out on a Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 (Brookhaven, UK). The confocal fluorescence imaging was performed with a ZEISS LSM780 confocal laser-scanning microscope (CLSM, Er Zeiss, Germany).

The CDs were synthetized by one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, quercetin (155.0 mg) and ethylenediamine (123.3 mg) were mixed in 6.0 mL of ultrapure water under ultrasonic for 5 min, and the mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor. Next, the reaction was performed in an oven at 200 °C for 12 h, and cooled to room temperature naturally. The precipitate was subsequently filtered through a membrane (0.22 μm) and purified via silica gel column chromatography with CH2Cl2/MeOH (V/V, 20/1) as the eluent solvents. Finally, the solvents were removed by rotary evaporation, and the product was re-dissolved with ultrapure water. The solution emits strong green fluorescence when illuminated with UV light of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of pH and acidic amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: The pH-sensitive characteristics of CDs were investigated. The fluorescence “off-on-off” assay of CDs was implemented in the PBS buffer by adjusting the pH in the range of 3.5 to 13.5 with HCl or NaOH. Meanwhile, the Asp and Glu solutions (0.01–0.5 mM) were prepared with ultrapure water. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were collected with the excitation wavelength of 400 nm.

The cytotoxicity of CDs was studied by the MTT assay. Firstly, the HepG2 cells were cultivated into 96-well plate until the cells were adhered to the cells wall. Subsequently, a various amounts of CDs (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 and 4.0 mg mL−1) were introduced and incubated for 24 h. Then, the MTT (10 μL, 5.0 mg mL−1) was added to each well, which were incubated for 8 h to form formazan dye and washed with PBS to remove floating MTT. Finally, DMSO (150 μL) was added, and the well plates were shaken at room temperature for 20 min to dissolve the formazan. The absorbance of the purple formazan was implemented on an enzyme-linked immune instrument at 490 nm.

The HepG2 cells were cultured in glass bottom dish for 24 h. The cells were then cultured with the fresh culture medium (1.0 mL) containing 1.2 mg mL−1 CDs for 6 h. Before imaging, the cells were washed several times with the PBS buffer to remove excess CDs, and then different pH values (4.5, 5.5, 6.5 and 7.4) were added to interact with cells for 10 min. Finally, the cellular fluorescence images were measured on a CLSM. The emission signals were collected from green channel. All images were obtained under constant parameters.

The fabrication route of CDs is shown in Scheme 1 . The CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using quercetin and ethylenediamine in ultrapure water (6 mL) at 200 °C for 12 h. The quercetin molecule contains two diphenol structures which are easily oxidized to form a quinoid structure. When ethylenediamine is added, the nucleophilic reaction (namely: Schiff base reaction) of the carbonyl group (C=O) on the molecular surface with the amino group (-NH2) easily occurs. At high temperature, a series of chemical reactions between molecules including Schiff base reaction and self-polymerization obtained CDs. To obtain the excellent optical properties and effective synthesis conditions of CDs, we studied the main factors influencing the reaction including temperature, time and the molar ratio of reactants. Consequently, the color of the product was observed under UV light (365 nm). Moreover, the spectral characteristics of CDs were further evaluated under different conditions on the spectrofluorophotometer. The optimum temperature was determined to be 200 °C (Fig. S1A). The reaction time of preparing CDs was 12 h at 200 °C (Fig. S1B). Afterward, the effect of the molar ratio of quercetin to ethylenediamine was studied. By observing the fluorescence intensity of the products, the best reaction molar ratio of 1:4 was obtained (Fig. S1C). For the purification of coarse products, most of the reported carbon dots purification methods are dialysis [48,49]. This method is mainly used to separate molecules of different sizes, but it cannot separate different luminescent substances, resulting in low yield. Therefore, it is very important to separate and purify as-prepared products. Based on the different polarity of solvents, silica gel column chromatography was used to purify the crude products with dichloromethane and methanol as the elution solvents. After removed the solvents, the obtained CDs were dispersed in water and bright green fluorescence was observed under UV light. It is exhibited that CDs have good water solubility. The CDs were stored in the refrigerator for about 100 days, and the fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged (Fig. S1D), illustrating that CDs are easy to be stored and have good stability in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The spectral properties of CDs including fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption were investigated. Two characteristic peaks are revealed in the absorption spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1A). In the characteristic absorption band of 200–300 nm, the absorption peak at 230 nm belongs to π–π* transition of CC in conjugated aromatic structure. Importantly, another characteristic absorption peak of CDs appears at 405 nm, which is attributed to the n–π* transitions of C=O/C=N. Thus, the absorption spectrum affirms the microstructure of CDs. In addition, Fig. 1A illustrates the excitation and emission spectra of CDs, exhibiting a maximum green emission at 510 nm with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. Unlike most other carbon dots, when the excitation wavelength was raised from 340 to 430 nm, the synthesized CDs exhibited excitation-independent behavior (Fig. 1B). The reason of excitation-independent phenomenon may be related to narrow size distribution of CDs and the effect of surface passivation function [50].

To further understand the luminescence properties of CDs, the quantum yield (QYs) of CDs was measured at 400 nm excitation and calculated to be about 13.4% using fluorescein as a standard in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4), which is higher than those of reports (Fig. S2) [51,52]. Under the same conditions, the QYs of the CDs were measured at pH 4.5 and 12.5, respectively. The calculated QYs were 0.32% and 0.37%, respectively. Thus, the CDs have higher luminescence efficiency at pH 7.4. Furthermore, the averaged lifetimes of CDs were studied. The photoluminescence (PL) decay curve of CDs is fitted by monoexponential functions, and averaged lifetime of 2.45 ns was obtained (Fig. S3A). To a certain extent, the luminescence principle of CDs may be caused by the radiation recombination nature of excitations [13]. The optical homogeneity of the CDs is extremely beneficial to exploring its intrinsic luminescence mechanism.

The morphology and structure of CDs were characterized by TEM, XPS, FT-IR, XRD, and Raman spectrum. The CDs have good dispersibility and similar spherical morphology, which was confirmed by the TEM (Fig. 1C). The lattice spacing of CDs is 0.25 nm in the HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1C), which is in accordance with the spacing between graphene (002) layers [53]. The average particle size of CDs is 2.7 ± 0.5 nm within the range from 1.5 to 4.5 nm (Fig. 1D). The surface groups of CDs were studied by the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3B). The absorption bands at 1656 and 3443 cm−1 are attributed to the characteristics absorption of C=O/C=N and N−H/O−H, respectively. The peak at 1384 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C−N. The FT-IR characterization confirmed that the surface of CDs may contain functional groups including amino and carboxyl groups.

Meanwhile, the surface groups and element composition of CDs were analyzed by XPS (Fig. S4). The peaks at 531.2, 399.4, and 284.4 eV appear in the XPS spectrum of CDs, corresponding to the elements of O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively (Fig. S4A). The survey spectrum illustrates that the CDs consist of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen, and the element percentages are 63.2%, 22.7%, and 14.1%, respectively. Four main peaks shown in the high resolution spectrum of C1s correspond to CC (283.9 eV), C−C (285.0 eV), C−O/C−N (286.1 eV) and C=O/C=N (288.7 eV) functional groups (Fig. S4B). The two peaks of O1s at 531.5 and 532.3 eV are attributed to the chemical functional groups of CO and C−OH/C−O−C, respectively (Fig. S4C). The N1s spectrum exhibits three nitrogen functional groups of N−H (401.5 eV), C−N−C (400.0 eV) and CN (399.1 eV) as shown in Fig. S4D. In addition, The XRD measurement was implemented (Fig. S5A). The CDs exhibit a diffraction peak centered at about 25.9°, which affirms that the (002) interlayer spacing distance of CDs is 0.34 nm. It is close to bulk graphite [54] and has good crystallization characteristics. The Raman spectrum shows two major features, a D band at 1385 cm−1 and a G band at 1578 cm−1, belonging to sp3 and sp2 hybrid carbons, respectively (Fig. S5B). The relative intensity of the disordered D band and crystalline G band (ID/IG) of CDs is around 0.88, indicating that its structure is similar to that of graphite [55]. The results affirm that the surface of CDs is rich in carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, and corresponding functional groups exist on the surface of CDs.

The pH-response properties of CDs were studied in the PBS solutions within the pH range of 3.5–13.5. The fluorescence signal enhanced gradually with increasing pH from 3.5 to 7.4 (Fig. 2A), and the color change of corresponding solution was observed from colorless to green under the UV light (365 nm) as shown in the inset of Fig. 2A. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F7.4) of CDs had a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.991) in the range of pH 3.5–7.4 (Fig. 2B) (where F represents the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the pH range of 3.5–7.0 and F7.4 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs at pH 7.4). In addition, when the pH value varied from 7.4 to 13.5, the fluorescence signal of CDs decreased continuously (Fig. 2C). And a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.991) was obtained by linear fitting between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F7.4) and pH value ranging from 7.4 to 13.5 (where F represents the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the pH range of 8.0 to 13.5). The fluorescent color of CDs solution is changed to colorless from green (Fig. 2C inset) under 365 nm UV light. It is confirmed that CDs possess the pH-sensitive characteristics and can be used as a novel nanoprobe for “off-on-off” monitoring of pH. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching and recovery times of CDs were also studied, respectively. The fluorescence quenching time is 420 s (Fig. S6A) and recovery time is 480 s (Fig. S6B). The photostability of luminescent nanomaterials in the detection system is a significant factor for evaluating its application. The photoreversible properties of CDs were further discussed in acid and alkali solution. As shown in Fig. 3A, when the pH changed from 7.4 to 4.5 and then to 7.4, the fluorescence intensity of CDs did not change much, and the corresponding color changed from green to colorless and then to green (Fig. 3A, inset). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CDs presented a good reproducibility by 4 cycles (Fig. 3B). Under the same experimental conditions, we also investigated the photoreversibility of pH between 7.4 and 12.5, when the pH varied from 7.4 to 12.5 and then to 7.4, the fluorescence intensity of CDs had little influence (Fig. 3C), and the corresponding fluorescence color of solution varied from green to colorless and then to green (Fig. 3C, inset). The CDs exhibited good fluorescence reversibility by 4 cycles (Fig. 3D). Furthermore, at different pH values (7.4, 4.5, and 12.5), the photostability of CDs was investigated by intermittent irradiation for 60 min under UV lamp (365 nm). The fluorescence intensity of CDs changed slightly at different pH (7.4, 4.5, and 12.5), which decreased by 0.85%, 6.1%, and 5.8% after UV lamp irradiation for 60 min, respectively (Fig. S6C, D). It can be seen that CDs still have good photostability in acid-base solution. Because CDs possess the properties of photostability and reversibility, presenting effective data for the development of CDs as a pH nanoprobe to detect pH level in environment. Furthermore, the intra-day and inter-day precision of CDs at different pH was studied, respectively. As shown in Table S1, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of CDs was determined through three-time measurements in intra-day at different pH values, and the RSDs are less than 2.0%. Meanwhile, the calculated RSDs are less than 4.0% within 5 days of inter-day. The results display that the intra-day and inter-day precisions are good, which is suitable for analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence “off-on-off” method for determination of pH
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM, Zeta-potential, and UV–Vis absorption spectrum were used to further explore the luminescence mechanism of CDs and to explain the effect of pH change on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. To affirm the pH-dependence and surface charge of CDs, we investigated the Zeta-potential of the CDs, which was −5.05 ± 0.46 mV at pH 7.4. In addition, at pH 4.5 and 12.5, the Zeta-potential of the CDs were −4.90 ± 0.29 and −4.52 ± 0.23 mV, respectively. Under other pH conditions, there was no obvious change in the Zeta-potential of CDs, indicating that the introduction of H+/OH− has little influence on the surface charge of CDs (Table S2). The experimental results proved that CDs possess negative charge, which may be due to the hydrogen bond interaction between the surface amino group and water, which interrupts the protonation and deprotonation process [56], making the surface carboxyl group dominating at the pH value of 4.5, 7.4, and 12.5. However, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs obviously change at pH values of 4.5, 7.4, and 12.5 (Fig. 4A). The absorption peak at 410 nm displays a red shift and decrease at pH value of 4.5 and 12.5, respectively, compared to that of the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs at pH 7.4. This may be due to the electron effect of the surface group, which leads to the red shift of the absorption peak [57]. The observed red shift of absorption peak may also be attributed to the formation of larger particles at pH values of 4.5 and 12.5. It is found that the average diameters of CDs at pH 4.5 and 12.5 are 6.8 ± 0.2 nm (Fig. 4B) and 6.4 ± 0.1 nm (Fig. 4C), respectively. Compared with the average particle diameter of CDs (2.7 ± 0.5 nm) at pH 7.4 (Fig. 1D), the average particle diameters of CDs were significantly increased at pH values of 4.5 and 12.5. In acid-base solution, the size increase of CDs may be due to the formation of hydrogen bonds by non-covalent bonds of carboxyl and amino groups [57]. Moreover, when the pH is changed from 7.4 to 4.5 or 7.4 to12.5, the color of solution obviously was changed to colorless from green under UV lamp (Fig. 4D). To deeply affirm the mechanism of pH-induced aggregation, we studied the dynamic light scattering of CDs at different pH values (4.5, 7.4, and 12.5). The results show that the particular size of CDs at pH 7.4 is smaller than that at other pH values (Fig. S7A). Moreover, the results are consistent with those obtained at the corresponding pH values based on the particle distribution of TEM data (Fig. S7B). Hence, we speculate that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs could be attributed to pH-induced aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs fluorescent pH nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: In bioanalytical detection, complex organisms containing some anions and amino acid biomolecules may interfere with the detection, so it is important to estimate the selectivity of the CDs fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 5A, in pH 7.4 PBS buffer, the effects of various foreign inorganic ions including 50 μM K+, Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, F−, PO4 3−, Cl−, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, and HCO3 − were studied, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs did not change obviously. In addition, the corresponding CDs solution color did not change under UV light (Fig. S8). At the same time, we also investigated the selectivity of CDs to exogenous ions in the PBS buffers with different pH values (Fig. S8). There was no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of CDs at pH 4.5, 5.5, and 6.5 (Fig. S9A, B and C), indicating that CDs have good selectivity against exogenous ions at different pH values. On the other hand, Fig. 5B authenticates that several exogenous common amino acids such as 50 μM tryptophan (Trp), leucine (Leu), glutamic acid (Glu), phenylalanine (Phe), lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg), alanine (Ala), asparagine (Asp), threonine (Thr), histidine (His), serine (Ser), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), and homocysteine (Hcy) did not affect the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Therefore, the CDs exhibited good selectivity against these exogenous ions and small biomolecules. The fluorescence intensity of CDs did not respond to NaCl (0–2.0 mol/L) under the same experimental conditions (Fig. S5B). These experimental results show that the high selectivity of CDs can meet the requirements for detection of analytes in biological system.

The CDs are pH-sensitive and can achieve “off-on-off” detection to pH in the range of 4.5 to 12.5. Therefore, we tried to use CDs as a pH-nanoprobe for the detection of acidic amino acids. Considering that the isoelectric points of Glu and Asp are 2.77 and 3.22, respectively, which meet the fluorescence response range of CDs to pH, we selected Glu and Asp as the analytes. Fig. 5A–F illustrates that the CDs have good fluorescence responses to two acidic amino acids. When different amounts of Asp and Glu (0.01–0.5 mM) were introduced in ultrapure water system, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreased (Fig. 5C and E). When 0.5 mM solutions of Asp and Glu were added, the quenching efficiency of the fluorescence intensity of CDs was 72.9% and 60.3%, respectively. Especially, the influence of different concentrations Asp and Glu on the performance of pH-nanoprobe were evaluated by Stern-Volmer equation. For Asp and Glu, the regression equation was (F/F0)510 nm = −5.71 [Asp] (mM) + 1.05, R2 = 0.993, (F/F0)510 nm = −4.20 [Glu] (mM) + 0.99, R2 = 0.991, respectively. The concentrations of Asp and Glu have a good linearity in the range of 0.01–0.1 mM with the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The detection limit (LOD) of Glu and Asp is determined to be 1.69 and 1.24 μM, severally (LOD = 3 s/k, s: standard deviation, k: slope) (Fig. 5D and F). Compared to the previous report [58], this method has a lower detection limit. The results indicated that CDs are a good pH-nanoprobe for fast response and has low detection concentrations for Glu and Asp in an acidic range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methylthiazol tetrazolium assay testing and intracellular pH imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The pH-sensitive property of CDs was used to detect intracellular pH levels. The cytotoxicity of CDs was analyzed by the methylthiazol tetrazolium (MTT) assay before cell imaging (Fig. S10). The cell viability was still 74.7% after treatment with high concentration of CDs (4.0 mg mL−1) for 24 h, indicating that the CDs have low cytotoxicity to cells. Therefore, The CDs can be effectively applied to intracellular imaging due to their good biocompatibility.

In order to further demonstrate that CDs can be applied to monitoring of pH level in living cells, cellular uptake time of CDs was examined using the CLSM. The HepG2 cells were treated with CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) at different time (1, 3, 6, and 10 h) (Fig. S11). When the cells were incubated with CDs for 6 h, a green highlights were clearly observed (Fig. S11A). Meanwhile, it was found that the fluorescence intensity increased with the extension of incubation time by scanning individual cells (Fig. S11B). Although a possible mechanism for the cellular uptake of CDs is not well understood, the cellular uptake of CDs is time-dependent. Secondly, the HepG2 cells were incubated with CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) for 6 h. The culture medium was washed with the PBS buffer. Afterward, the cells were treated at pH 4.5–7.4 for 10 min. Fig. 6A shows that the green fluorescence in the cell gradually becomes bright when pH is raised from 4.5 to 7.4. In particular, when the cells were treated at pH 4.5 and 5.0, the green fluorescence was hardly observed (Fig. 6A(A1, A2)), however, when the cells were treated in the pH range of 5.5 to 7.4, bright green fluorescence enhanced gradually (Fig. 6A(A3–A7)). Similarly, the fluorescence intensity was gradually enhanced by scanning individual cells with pH changed from 4.5 to 7.4 (Fig. 6B). The imaging results were consistent with the observation of the change of fluorescence color of CDs solution in the pH range of 4.5–7.4 under the UV light (365 nm). Furthermore, the green fluorescence of CDs may effectively avoid the interference of self-fluorescence in biological matrix. It is exhibited that the nanoprobe is sensitive to the intracellular pH change and can be developed for monitoring intracellular pH levels.

In summary, the CDs have been fabricated by a hydrothermal method with quercetin and ethylenediamine as raw materials. The green fluorescent CDs were obtained by purification using column chromatography. The structural characterization and spectral properties of CDs have been studied. The CDs exhibited excitation-independent property and have the advantages such as good photostability, photoreversibility and biocompatibility. The fluorescence intensity ratio of CDs has good linearity in the range of pH 3.5–13.5, and it realized “off-on-off” monitoring of pH value. Moreover, the pH-dependence mechanism of CDs was further studied. It is explained that the fluorescence quenching is due to the pH-induced aggregation of CDs. Meanwhile, the pH-nanoprobe was applied to detect of acidic amino acids, and the results were satisfactory. More importantly, CDs have good biocompatibility and are exploited for intracellular pH imaging. It is further demonstrated that CDs are a promising biological nanoprobe for real-time tracking of intracellular pH levels, which is expected to be useful in biomedicine, disease diagnosis and biomarkers.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.110401.
